title: Friedrich August Wolf

infobox.value: ([/ˈvɔːlf/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English");[[1]](#cite_note-1) German: [[vɔlf]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German"); 15 February 1759 – 8 August 1824) was a German [classicist](/wiki/Classicist "Classicist") who is considered the founder of [classical](/wiki/Classics#Philology "Classics") and modern [philology](/wiki/Philology "Philology").

[Thompson Klein, Julie](/wiki/Julie_Thompson_Klein "Julie Thompson Klein") (1 February 2012). "1". [*Humanities, Culture, and Interdisciplinarity: The Changing American Academy*](https://books.google.com/books?id=75kyfNSw8u4C). SUNY Press. p. 27. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-7914-8267-4](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-7914-8267-4 "Special:BookSources/978-0-7914-8267-4"). Retrieved 21 December 2016.

Tate, Aaron Phillip (2010). ["Herder, Heyne, and F.A. Wolf: An Homeric Controversy and its Relevance Today"](http://www.ssrn.com/abstract=1608207). *SSRN Electronic Journal*. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.2139/ssrn.1608207](https://doi.org/10.2139%2Fssrn.1608207). [ISSN](/wiki/ISSN_(identifier) "ISSN (identifier)") [1556-5068](https://search.worldcat.org/issn/1556-5068).

S. Krmnicek und M. Gaidys, Gelehrtenbilder. [Altertumswissenschaftler auf Medaillen des 19. Jahrhunderts](http://hdl.handle.net/10900/100742). Begleitband zur [online-Ausstellung im Digitalen Münzkabinett des Instituts für Klassische Archäologie der Universität Tübingen](http://online-Ausstellung%20im%20Digitalen%20Münzkabinett%20des%20Instituts%20für%20Klassische%20Archäologie%20der%20Universität%20Tübingen), in: S. Krmnicek (Hrsg.), Von Krösus bis zu König Wilhelm. Neue Serie Bd. 3 (Tübingen 2020), 69-71.

Friedrich Schleiermacher, "Ueber den Begriff der Hermeneutik mit Bezug auf F. A. Wolfs Andeutungen und Asts Lehrbuch", lecture delivered on 13 August 1829; published in *Friedrich Schleiermachers sämtliche Werke* III/3, 1838 (Schleiermacher makes reference to Ast's *Grundlinien der Grammatik, Hermeneutik und Kritik* (1808) and Wolf's *Vorlesungen über die Enzyklopädie der Altertumswissenschaft* (1831)); Richard E. Palmer, *Hermeneutics*, Northwestern University Press, 1969, ch. 6.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

German philologist (1759–1824)

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article includes a list of [general references](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#General_references "Wikipedia:Citing sources"), but **it lacks sufficient corresponding [inline citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Inline_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")**. Please help to [improve](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Reliability "Wikipedia:WikiProject Reliability") this article by [introducing](/wiki/Wikipedia:When_to_cite "Wikipedia:When to cite") more precise citations. *(January 2013)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Portrait_of_Friedrich_August_Wolf_%281823%29.jpg/250px-Portrait_of_Friedrich_August_Wolf_%281823%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_Friedrich_August_Wolf_(1823).jpg)

Portrait by [Johann Eduard Wolff](/w/index.php?title=Johann_Eduard_Wolff&action=edit&redlink=1 "Johann Eduard Wolff (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Johann_Eduard_Wolff "de:Johann Eduard Wolff")], 1823

**Friedrich August Wolf** ([/ˈvɔːlf/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English");[[1]](#cite_note-1) German: [[vɔlf]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German"); 15 February 1759 – 8 August 1824) was a German [classicist](/wiki/Classicist "Classicist") who is considered the founder of [classical](/wiki/Classics#Philology "Classics") and modern [philology](/wiki/Philology "Philology").

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Friedrich_August_Wolf&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

Wolf was born in [Hainrode](/wiki/Hainrode "Hainrode"), near [Nordhausen](/wiki/Nordhausen,_Thuringia "Nordhausen, Thuringia"). His father was the village schoolmaster and [organist](/wiki/Organist "Organist"). In [grammar school](/wiki/Grammar_school "Grammar school"), he studied [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language") and [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") as well as [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), and [music](/wiki/Music "Music").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonro1911770-2)

In 1777, after two years of independent study, at the age of eighteen, Wolf went to the [University of Göttingen](/wiki/University_of_G%C3%B6ttingen "University of Göttingen").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonro1911770-2) Legend has it that he chose to enroll in the department of "[philology](/wiki/Philology "Philology")", despite the fact that the university had none. His enrollment was nonetheless accepted as submitted.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] At the time [Christian Gottlob Heyne](/wiki/Christian_Gottlob_Heyne "Christian Gottlob Heyne") was a member of the faculty. Heyne excluded Wolf from his lectures, and criticized Wolf's views on [Homer](/wiki/Homer "Homer"). Wolf was undeterred and pursued his studies through the university's library.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonro1911770–771-3)

From 1779 to 1783, he taught at [Ilfeld](/wiki/Ilfeld "Ilfeld") and [Osterode](/wiki/Ostr%C3%B3da "Ostróda"). He published an edition of *[Plato's Symposium](/wiki/Symposium_(Plato) "Symposium (Plato)")*, and in 1783, he was awarded a chair at the [University of Halle](/wiki/University_of_Halle "University of Halle") in [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia").[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonro1911771-4)

It was in [Halle](/wiki/Halle,_Saxony-Anhalt "Halle, Saxony-Anhalt") (1783–1807), with the support of ministers serving under [Frederick the Great](/wiki/Frederick_the_Great "Frederick the Great"), that Wolf first laid down the principles of the field he would call "Philology".[[5]](#cite_note-Klein2012-5) He defined philology as the study of [human nature](/wiki/Human_nature "Human nature") as exhibited in antiquity. Its methods include the examination of the history, writing, art and other examples of ancient cultures. It combines the study of history and language, through interpretation, in which history and linguistics coalesce into an organic whole. This was the ideal of Wolf's philological *seminarium* at Halle.

During Wolf's time at Halle he published his [commentary](/wiki/Commentary_(philology) "Commentary (philology)") on the *[Leptines](/wiki/Leptines "Leptines")* of [Demosthenes](/wiki/Demosthenes "Demosthenes") (1789), which influenced his student [Philipp August Böckh](/wiki/Philipp_August_B%C3%B6ckh "Philipp August Böckh").[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonro1911771-4) He also published the *[Prolegomena ad Homerum](/w/index.php?title=Prolegomena_ad_Homerum&action=edit&redlink=1 "Prolegomena ad Homerum (page does not exist)")* (1795), which led to accusations of plagiarism by Heyne.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Medaille_Friedrich_August_Wolf_1840.png/250px-Medaille_Friedrich_August_Wolf_1840.png)](/wiki/File:Medaille_Friedrich_August_Wolf_1840.png)

Medal Friedrich August Wolf (1840)

The Halle professorship ended after the [French invasion of 1806](/wiki/Battle_of_Halle "Battle of Halle"). He relocated to [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), where he received assistance from [Wilhelm von Humboldt](/wiki/Wilhelm_von_Humboldt "Wilhelm von Humboldt"). Later he once more took a professorship, but he no longer taught with his old success, and he wrote very little. His most finished work, the *Darstellung der Alterthumswissenschaft*, though published
at Berlin (1807), belongs essentially to the Halle time.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonro1911771-4)

Taking medical advice, Wolf travelled to the south but died on the road to [Marseille](/wiki/Marseille "Marseille"), and was buried there. In 1840 a medal was struck in his honor.[[7]](#cite_note-7)
